name: John Cale
description:
'''John Davies Cale''' (born 9 March 1942) is a Welsh singer, musician, composer, record producer and arranger. He is a founding member of the influential American [[Rock music|rock]] band [[the Velvet Underground]], with whom he recorded two studio albums. Over his six-decade career, Cale has worked in various styles of rock and [[avant-garde music]].

John Cale studied music at [[Goldsmiths, University of London|Goldsmiths College, University of London]] before moving in 1963 to [[New York City]], where he performed as part of the [[Drone music|drone]] collective the [[Theatre of Eternal Music]] and formed the Velvet Underground. Since leaving the band in 1968, Cale has released seventeen solo [[Album#Studio|studio albums]], including ''[[Paris 1919 (album)|Paris 1919]]'' (1973) which is retrospectively considered to be his [[masterpiece]], ''[[Fear (John Cale album)|Fear]]'' (1974), ''[[Slow Dazzle (album)|Slow Dazzle]]'' (1975) and ''[[Music for a New Society]]'' (1982). In 1990, he collaborated with Velvet Underground co-founder [[Lou Reed]] on ''[[Songs for Drella]]'' which was a tribute for their mentor [[Andy Warhol]]. 

Cale has worked as a record producer on studio albums by artists including [[Nico]], [[the Stooges]], [[the Modern Lovers]], [[Patti Smith]], [[Squeeze (band)|Squeeze]], [[Happy Mondays]] and [[Siouxsie and the Banshees]]. Cale was inducted into the [[Rock and Roll Hall of Fame]] as a member of the Velvet Underground in 1996.
